title: Classification results for two-dimensional Lagrangian tori
speaker: Georgios Dimitroglou-Rizell
abstract: We present several classification results for Lagrangian tori, all proven using the splitting construction from symplectic field theory. Notably, we classify Lagrangian tori in the symplectic vector space up to Hamiltonian isotopy; they are either product tori or rescalings of the Chekanov torus. The proof uses the following results established in a recent joint work with E. Goodman and A. Ivrii. First, there is a unique torus up to Lagrangian isotopy inside the symplectic vector space, the projective plane, as well as the monotone $S^2 \times S^2$.

proof_stages.label: Rigidity background and h-principle failures
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates classification of Lagrangian tori by reviewing rigidity phenomena, exact cotangent-bundle restrictions, and the Clifford/Chekanov and Vianna sources of non-Hamiltonian-isotopic tori.

proof_stages.label: Main Lagrangian and Hamiltonian classification statements
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states that homotopic two-dimensional Lagrangian tori in the principal four-dimensional ambient manifolds are Lagrangian isotopic, gives the exact T^*T^2 Hamiltonian corollary, and states the R^4 Hamiltonian classification by product and Chekanov tori.

proof_stages.label: Weinstein neighborhood and adapted almost-complex structures
proof_stages.summary: A Weinstein cotangent-disk neighborhood of the Lagrangian torus is fixed with angular and momentum coordinates, together with standard and cylindrical almost-complex structures tailored to the flat unit cotangent bundle.

proof_stages.label: Neck stretching and SFT holomorphic buildings
proof_stages.summary: The cylindrical collar is stretched, Gromov foliations of minimal-area spheres are followed through the stretching, and SFT compactness produces punctured holomorphic buildings with Reeb asymptotics given by lifts of flat geodesics on the torus.

proof_stages.label: Broken-leaf analysis and divisor extraction
proof_stages.summary: Index, area, and positivity-of-intersection arguments classify the generic and exceptional broken leaves, show that generic leaves remain unbroken in the complement, yield a pseudoholomorphic divisor, and rule out forbidden broken-small-plane configurations.

proof_stages.label: Compact small-plane moduli and solid torus
proof_stages.summary: The exclusion of bad breakings makes the moduli of small planes compact, and automatic transversality plus asymptotic intersection theory identify the asymptotic-evaluation map as a diffeomorphism whose universal curve compactifies to a solid torus foliated by symplectic discs.

proof_stages.label: Solid-torus construction of Lagrangian isotopy
proof_stages.summary: The solid torus gives a smooth radial contraction, and inflation supplies the extra symplectic room needed to correct the characteristic-flow obstruction and promote this to a Lagrangian isotopy.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian upgrade via flux correction and conic reduction
proof_stages.summary: Flux changes along a Lagrangian isotopy are corrected by suitable non-Hamiltonian symplectomorphisms, and in R^4 a pseudoholomorphic conic linking the torus in the Clifford or Chekanov pattern is made standard by Gromov before translations and rescalings yield the Hamiltonian classification.

examples.name: Luttinger torus spin of a knot
examples.note: The torus spin construction from a knot in R^3 illustrates the sharp contrast between flexible totally real tori and rigid Lagrangian tori, since a Lagrangian representative exists only for the unknot.
examples.engagement_reason: The Luttinger torus spin is roughly described and its iff-unknot theorem is stated, but no construction or property is worked through on a specific knot.

passes.reason: Non-unknot torus spins are ordinary knotted embedded tori used to show the totally-real/h-principle expectation fails for Lagrangian embeddings.

Used to show the Lagrangian h-principle fails, since non-unknot torus spins can be totally real but not Lagrangian.
Torus spins of nontrivial knots form a systematic family and are used to exhibit failure of the Lagrangian h-principle.
nontrivial torus spins are ordinary knotted-torus examples used to show the totally-real/formal h-principle need not give a Lagrangian representative
A nontrivial knot-spun torus is part of a systematic knotted family and is used as a totally real torus that cannot be Lagrangian, refuting a Lagrangian h-principle.

Luttinger’s torus spins of nontrivial knots are used to show the Lagrangian h-principle fails, but the spun tori form a systematic family rather than an extremal, degenerate, symmetric, or sporadic object.

examples.name: Vianna's monotone exotic tori
examples.note: The infinite families of monotone Lagrangian tori in CP^2 and S^2 x S^2 are used to illustrate why Hamiltonian classification in closed manifolds is subtler than Lagrangian isotopy classification.
examples.engagement_reason: Vianna’s exotic tori are not only cited but the speaker applies the classification/ball-placement result to conclude how their Hamiltonian exoticity disappears when allowed to move in the ball or R^4.

passes.reason: Vianna's tori form infinite non-sporadic monotone families used to refute Hamiltonian-uniqueness or classical-invariant classification.
an infinite systematic family of exotic monotone tori used as an obstruction to naive Hamiltonian classification
Used as infinitely many monotone tori with the same classical flavor but distinct Hamiltonian isotopy classes, obstructing naive Hamiltonian classification.
Vianna's tori are an infinite non-sporadic family used to show Hamiltonian uniqueness/classification fails in closed symplectic manifolds.
Vianna’s tori form infinite exotic families rather than sporadic exceptions and are cited as failures of Hamiltonian uniqueness in closed symplectic manifolds
Vianna’s tori form an infinite exotic family, not sporadic exceptions, and are cited to show monotone tori need not be Hamiltonian isotopic.

Vianna’s monotone exotic tori obstruct Hamiltonian-isotopy classification in closed symplectic manifolds, but they belong to an infinite systematic family and are not exceptional in the sporadic sense.

Vianna’s exotic tori are invoked as infinitely many monotone tori not Hamiltonian isotopic, obstructing Hamiltonian uniqueness, but they are not sporadic exceptional objects in the classification sense.
Vianna's exotic monotone tori obstruct Hamiltonian uniqueness from classical data, but they are an infinite family rather than sporadic or extremal objects.
Used as exotic monotone tori obstructing Hamiltonian uniqueness/classification, but they occur in infinite systematic families rather than as sporadic exceptions.
Used as infinitely many monotone tori obstructing Hamiltonian-isotopy uniqueness in closed symplectic manifolds.
Vianna's tori are an infinite exotic family used to refute Hamiltonian-isotopy uniqueness/classification by classical invariants.

examples.name: Riemann-surface annulus analogy for neck stretching
examples.note: Replacing an annulus in a Riemann surface by one of larger modulus is given as a familiar two-dimensional analogy for stretching a symplectization neck.
examples.engagement_reason: The Riemann-surface annulus is used only as an analogy for neck stretching—excise an annulus and replace it by one of larger modulus—without further derivation.

examples.name: Higher-dimensional monotone tori as a limitation case
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker contrasts the four-dimensional Lagrangian-isotopy argument with earlier smooth-isotopy restrictions for monotone tori in C^N or CP^N, illustrating the dimension-specific nature of the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: Higher-dimensional monotone tori are invoked as a limitation and comparison case, with only general comments about failures of the method and prior results.

passes.reason: Higher-dimensional monotone tori are ordinary family cases invoked as an obstruction to extending the four-dimensional isotopy method.
